verses.text: Now the sons of Issachar were, Tola, and Puah, Jashub, and Shimron, four. And the sons of Tola; Uzzi, and Rephaiah, and Jeriel, and Jahmai, and Jibsam, and Shemuel, heads of their father's house, to wit, of Tola: they were valiant men of might in their generations; whose number was in the days of David two and twenty thousand and six hundred. And the sons of Uzzi; Izrahiah: and the sons of Izrahiah; Michael, and Obadiah, and Joel, Ishiah, five: all of them chief men. And with them, by their generations, after the house of their fathers, were bands of soldiers for war, six and thirty thousand men: for they had many wives and sons. And their brethren among all the families of Issachar were valiant men of might, reckoned in all by their genealogies fourscore and seven thousand. Guidance: The Chronicler turns to the northern tribes, beginning with Issachar, whose families are recorded as 'valiant men of might,' numbering 87,000 fighting men in the days of David. Issachar was the tribe of which it was said elsewhere that they 'had understanding of the times, to know what Israel ought to do' (1 Chron 12:32) — a people of both strength and wisdom. The chapter is showing us that the tribe of Issachar, though it produced no king and little fame, was a strong and substantial part of Israel, full of valiant men. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's people are not only their famous tribes; the body of Israel was made up of many such tribes of sturdy, faithful families, each contributing its strength to the whole. The chapter is teaching the post-exilic community that every tribe, even those that left little mark on the headlines of sacred history, was numerous, valiant, and counted among the people of God. The careful census numbers testify that the LORD knew His people not as a vague mass but tribe by tribe, family by family, man by man. The chapter is teaching us that there is honour in being a 'valiant man of might' in one's generation even without renown — to be strong, faithful, and ready to serve in one's appointed place is itself a noble calling, recorded and remembered by God.

The sons of Benjamin; Bela, and Becher, and Jediael, three. And the sons of Bela; Ezbon, and Uzzi, and Uzziel, and Jerimoth, and Iri, five; heads of the house of their fathers, mighty men of valour; and were reckoned by their genealogies twenty and two thousand and thirty and four. And the sons of Becher; Zemira, and Joash, and Eliezer, and Elioenai, and Omri, and Jerimoth, and Abiah, and Anathoth, and Alameth. All these are the sons of Becher. And the number of them, after their genealogy by their generations, heads of the house of their fathers, mighty men of valour, was twenty thousand and two hundred. The sons also of Jediael; Bilhan: and the sons of Bilhan; Jeush, and Benjamin, and Ehud, and Chenaanah, and Zethan, and Tharshish, and Ahishahar. All these the sons of Jediael, by the heads of their fathers, mighty men of valour, were seventeen thousand and two hundred soldiers, fit to go out for war and battle. Shuppim also, and Huppim, the children of Ir, and Hushim, the sons of Aher. Guidance: The Chronicler gives a first notice of Benjamin (he will return to this tribe at much greater length in chapters 8 and 9), recording its families as 'mighty men of valour,' fit for war and battle. Benjamin was a small tribe — nearly destroyed in the dark days of the Judges (Judg 20) — yet here it stands, numerous and strong, a tribe of warriors. The chapter is showing us the LORD's preservation and restoration of a tribe that had once come to the very brink of extinction; the Benjamin that had been reduced to six hundred men rose again to tens of thousands of mighty men of valour. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD can revive and multiply what has been nearly destroyed; no diminished people is beyond His power to restore. The chapter is teaching the post-exilic remnant — themselves a small and battered company — a quiet word of hope: the God who brought Benjamin back from the edge of annihilation to become a mighty tribe could likewise restore the returned exiles. The extra attention given to Benjamin throughout these chapters reflects its special place: it was one of the two tribes (with Judah) that remained loyal to the house of David and returned from exile to form the heart of the restored community. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD remembers and rebuilds the small and the wounded among His people, raising up mighty men from a tribe that once had almost none.

The sons of Naphtali; Jahziel, and Guni, and Jezer, and Shallum, the sons of Bilhah. Guidance: In a single short verse, the Chronicler records the sons of Naphtali — just four names, 'the sons of Bilhah' (Bilhah was Rachel's maid, the mother of Dan and Naphtali). After the long census-laden notices of other tribes, Naphtali receives only this brief mention. The chapter is showing us that even the tribe with the scantiest record is not omitted; Naphtali too is named and counted among the sons of Israel. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD forgets none of His people, even those of whom little is recorded. Where the sources were sparse, the Chronicler still set down what he had, unwilling to let any tribe drop out of the family of God. The chapter is teaching us a tender truth: in the LORD's reckoning, the obscure and the briefly-mentioned have their place as surely as the famous and the numerous. Naphtali's four names stand as a testimony that no son of Israel falls through the cracks of God's memory. And for the Christian reader there is a further note of grace: it was the land of Naphtali, this least-noticed tribe, upon which Isaiah promised a great light would shine — fulfilled when Jesus made Galilee of the nations, the region of Zebulun and Naphtali, the very seat of His ministry (Isa 9:1-2; Matt 4:13-16). The LORD honoured the obscure tribe at the last. None of His people is too small to be remembered, or too overlooked to be blessed.

The sons of Manasseh; Ashriel, whom she bare: (but his concubine the Aramitess bare Machir the father of Gilead: And Machir took to wife the sister of Huppim and Shuppim, whose sister's name was Maachah;) and the name of the second was Zelophehad: and Zelophehad had daughters. And Maachah the wife of Machir bare a son, and she called his name Peresh; and the name of his brother was Sheresh; and his sons were Ulam and Rakem. And the sons of Ulam; Bedan. These were the sons of Gilead, the son of Machir, the son of Manasseh. And his sister Hammoleketh bare Ishod, and Abiezer, and Mahalah. And the sons of Shemidah were, Ahian, and Shechem, and Likhi, and Aniam. Guidance: In the genealogy of Manasseh, through Machir and Gilead, the Chronicler pauses on a name worth remembering: 'Zelophehad had daughters.' Zelophehad died in the wilderness leaving no sons, only daughters — and those daughters (Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah) had famously come before Moses to plead that their father's name and inheritance not be lost for want of a son, and the LORD Himself ruled in their favour, granting them their father's portion (Num 27:1-11; 36). The chapter is showing us, by recording that 'Zelophehad had daughters,' the LORD's care for the rights and the place of women among His people. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD is a God of justice who does not overlook the vulnerable: when daughters had no brother to carry the inheritance, He made provision for them, and their bold, faithful appeal became part of the law of Israel. The chapter is teaching us that in the LORD's economy, daughters are not forgotten; their father's name was preserved through them, and their portion in the land was secured by divine decree. The brief notice that follows — that Hammoleketh, a woman, 'bare' sons, and the careful tracing of the line — shows again the Chronicler's attentiveness to the whole family, women included. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's concern reaches to every member of His covenant people, and that He upholds the cause of those whom a careless world might pass over. The daughters of Zelophehad stand in the genealogy as a lasting witness that faith which appeals to God is heard, and that He defends the inheritance of the overlooked.

And the sons of Ephraim; Shuthelah, and Bered his son, and Tahath his son, and Eladah his son, and Tahath his son, And Zabad his son, and Shuthelah his son, and Ezer, and Elead, whom the men of Gath that were born in that land slew, because they came down to take away their cattle. And Ephraim their father mourned many days, and his brethren came to comfort him. And when he went in to his wife, she conceived, and bare a son, and he called his name Beriah, because it went evil with his house. (And his daughter was Sherah, who built Bethhoron the nether, and the upper, and Uzzensherah.) And Rephah was his son, also Resheph, and Telah his son, and Tahan his son, Laadan his son, Ammihud his son, Elishama his son, Non his son, Jehoshua his son. Guidance: The genealogy of Ephraim opens unexpectedly into one of the most human and tender passages in all these lists. Ephraim's sons Ezer and Elead were killed by the men of Gath in a cattle raid, and 'Ephraim their father mourned many days, and his brethren came to comfort him.' Here is grief — a father weeping over slain children — set right in the middle of the dry genealogy, and here is the comfort of community, brethren coming to mourn alongside him. Then comes the LORD's gentle renewal: Ephraim's wife conceived again and bore a son named Beriah, 'because it went evil with his house' — a name remembering the sorrow, yet a new life given in the place of loss. The chapter is showing us that the LORD sees and records the private griefs of His people; the tears of a bereaved father are written into Scripture, not passed over. The chapter is teaching us that sorrow and loss are part of the story of God's people, and that the LORD does not despise our mourning — He gives the comfort of others ('his brethren came to comfort him') and, in His time, fresh mercies after grief (the birth of Beriah). The chapter is teaching us to grieve honestly and to comfort one another, and to trust that the LORD can bring new life out of the place of death. Two more wonders close the passage. First, 'his daughter was Sherah, who built Bethhoron the nether, and the upper, and Uzzensherah' — a woman who founded and built three cities, honoured in the record as a builder, a reminder that the LORD raises up capable women to do great and lasting work. And second, the line runs down at last to 'Non his son, Jehoshua his son' — Joshua the son of Nun, the great conqueror who led Israel into the promised land, Ephraim's most illustrious descendant. The chapter is teaching us that out of a family that knew bitter grief, the LORD brought forth a builder of cities and the deliverer of His people — that the line marked by sorrow became the line of Joshua. The LORD weaves His grandest purposes through the tears and the ordinary faithfulness of His people.

The chapter is teaching us that sorrow and loss are part of the story of God's people, and that the LORD does not despise our mourning — He gives the comfort of others ('his brethren came to comfort him') and, in His time, fresh mercies after grief (the birth of Beriah). The chapter is teaching us to grieve honestly and to comfort one another, and to trust that the LORD can bring new life out of the place of death. Two more wonders close the passage. First, 'his daughter was Sherah, who built Bethhoron the nether, and the upper, and Uzzensherah' — a woman who founded and built three cities, honoured in the record as a builder, a reminder that the LORD raises up capable women to do great and lasting work. And second, the line runs down at last to 'Non his son, Jehoshua his son' — Joshua the son of Nun, the great conqueror who led Israel into the promised land, Ephraim's most illustrious descendant. The chapter is teaching us that out of a family that knew bitter grief, the LORD brought forth a builder of cities and the deliverer of His people — that the line marked by sorrow became the line of Joshua. The LORD weaves His grandest purposes through the tears and the ordinary faithfulness of His people.

And their possessions and habitations were, Bethel and the towns thereof, and eastward Naaran, and westward Gezer, with the towns thereof; Shechem also and the towns thereof, unto Gaza and the towns thereof: And by the borders of the children of Manasseh, Bethshean and her towns, Taanach and her towns, Megiddo and her towns, Dor and her towns. In these dwelt the children of Joseph the son of Israel. Guidance: The Chronicler records the towns and territory of the children of Joseph — Ephraim and Manasseh — the central highlands of the land: Bethel, Shechem, Megiddo, Beth-shean, and the rest. This was the heartland of Israel, rich and storied ground. The chapter is showing us the inheritance the LORD gave to the house of Joseph, the place where these tribes dwelt in the land of promise. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD is faithful to settle His people in their inheritance; the towns named here are the fulfillment of the promise of land made to the patriarchs. Yet there is a quiet poignancy for the reader who knows the later history: this central territory became the heart of the breakaway northern kingdom, and these very tribes were the first carried away by Assyria, their towns lost to the people of God. The chapter is teaching us, by simply naming what once was theirs, both the generosity of the LORD's gift and the sorrow of an inheritance forfeited through unfaithfulness. For the post-exilic community, reading of Bethel and Shechem and Megiddo — towns no longer in their hands — the record was a reminder of how much had been lost, and perhaps a quiet hope of what the LORD might yet restore. The chapter is teaching us to receive the LORD's gifts with gratitude and to hold them with faithfulness, lest the inheritance once enjoyed be lost.

The sons of Asher; Imnah, and Isuah, and Ishuai, and Beriah, and Serah their sister. And the sons of Beriah; Heber, and Malchiel, who is the father of Birzavith. And Heber begat Japhlet, and Shomer, and Hotham, and Shua their sister. And the sons of Japhlet; Pasach, and Bimhal, and Ashvath. These are the children of Japhlet. And the sons of Shamer; Ahi, and Rohgah, Jehubbah, and Aram. And the sons of his brother Helem; Zophah, and Imna, and Shelesh, and Amal. The sons of Zophah; Suah, and Harnepher, and Shual, and Beri, and Imrah, Bezer, and Hod, and Shamma, and Shilshah, and Ithran, and Beera. And the sons of Jether; Jephunneh, and Pispah, and Ara. And the sons of Ulla; Arah, and Haniel, and Rezia. All these were the children of Asher, heads of their father's house, choice and mighty men of valour, chief of the princes. And the number throughout the genealogy of them that were apt to the war and to battle was twenty and six thousand men. Guidance: The chapter closes with the genealogy of Asher, whose families are summed up in a fine phrase: 'choice and mighty men of valour, chief of the princes,' some 26,000 men fit for war. Asher was one of the lesser tribes, dwelling in the far north along the coast, easily overlooked — yet the Chronicler honours it with high praise: choice men, mighty men, princes. The chapter is showing us that the LORD's commendation falls even on the small and remote tribes; Asher, far from Jerusalem and little mentioned in the great events of the history, is here dignified as a tribe of excellent, valiant men. The chapter is teaching us that worth in the LORD's eyes is not measured by prominence or proximity to the center; a distant tribe can be full of 'choice' men, princes in their own right. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD takes note of the quiet excellence of His people wherever they are found — the faithful strength of a northern coastal tribe is recorded as carefully as the deeds of kings. Even the inclusion of 'Serah their sister' (named also in Genesis and Numbers, a woman remembered across the generations) shows again the Chronicler's care to record the whole family. The chapter is teaching us, as it draws the survey of the tribes toward its close, that the whole of Israel — Issachar's valiant thousands, restored Benjamin, little-noticed Naphtali, grieving and triumphant Ephraim, and choice, princely Asher — all together made up the one people of God, each tribe known, numbered, and honoured by the LORD who keeps the record of His own.

The sons of Zophah; Suah, and Harnepher, and Shual, and Beri, and Imrah, Bezer, and Hod, and Shamma, and Shilshah, and Ithran, and Beera. And the sons of Jether; Jephunneh, and Pispah, and Ara. And the sons of Ulla; Arah, and Haniel, and Rezia. All these were the children of Asher, heads of their father's house, choice and mighty men of valour, chief of the princes. And the number throughout the genealogy of them that were apt to the war and to battle was twenty and six thousand men. Guidance: The chapter closes with the genealogy of Asher, whose families are summed up in a fine phrase: 'choice and mighty men of valour, chief of the princes,' some 26,000 men fit for war. Asher was one of the lesser tribes, dwelling in the far north along the coast, easily overlooked — yet the Chronicler honours it with high praise: choice men, mighty men, princes. The chapter is showing us that the LORD's commendation falls even on the small and remote tribes; Asher, far from Jerusalem and little mentioned in the great events of the history, is here dignified as a tribe of excellent, valiant men. The chapter is teaching us that worth in the LORD's eyes is not measured by prominence or proximity to the center; a distant tribe can be full of 'choice' men, princes in their own right. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD takes note of the quiet excellence of His people wherever they are found — the faithful strength of a northern coastal tribe is recorded as carefully as the deeds of kings. Even the inclusion of 'Serah their sister' (named also in Genesis and Numbers, a woman remembered across the generations) shows again the Chronicler's care to record the whole family. The chapter is teaching us, as it draws the survey of the tribes toward its close, that the whole of Israel — Issachar's valiant thousands, restored Benjamin, little-noticed Naphtali, grieving and triumphant Ephraim, and choice, princely Asher — all together made up the one people of God, each tribe known, numbered, and honoured by the LORD who keeps the record of His own.
